definition: The gap between standard and effective (labor-force-participation-adjusted) dependency ratios delays fiscal tipping points by 3-5 years. US 65-74 LFPR rose from 17% to 27% (2000-2024), shifting the effective 35% dependency crossing from ~2030-2032 to ~2033-2037. Whether this buffer is stable or procyclical (reversing during recessions) is untested.
evidence: Driven by: rising education, declining physical demands, better health, inadequate retirement savings
Shifts US 35% effective dependency crossing from ~2030-2032 to ~2033-2037
Part-time/lower-productivity older workers contribute less fiscal revenue
Disability rates declining among 65-74 (60% to 72% reporting no disability, 1990-2020)
Education levels rising (15% to 30% with bachelor's, 2000-2024)
Crude adjustment: 4-5pp lower effective dependency than headline
open_questions: Is the older worker LFPR increase stable or procyclical (reversing during asset price declines)?
Does the buffer disappear precisely when it is most needed (during recessions)?
How much does part-time/lower-productivity work reduce the economic significance of the gap?
Does the effective dependency gap plateau at older ages?
How much fiscal revenue do part-time/low-productivity older workers actually contribute?
Is the 5-10pp upper bound estimate supportable?
How should effective ratios be incorporated into fiscal tipping-point frameworks across OECD?
